macro_segments.title: Placing an Eruv in a Tree: The Basic Mishnah and Rava's Principle of Four Amot

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: Eruv Placement in Trees Above Ten Tefachim
The Problem of Reshus HaYachid and Reshus HaRabbim Separation
Rava's Position: The Four Amot Principle and Virtual Space Compression
Explanation of Ibura Shel Ir (Environs of the City) and Its Application

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Challenge to Rava: If Space Compression Applies, Why Not Everywhere?

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Logical Question: Space Compression Should Apply Outside Ibura as Well
Rava's Justification: Four Amot Around the Eruv Is Like a Private Domain
The Gemara's Reframing: All Eruv Placement Should Be Permitted Based on This Logic

macro_segments.title: The Extenuating Case: A Tree Branch Extending Beyond Four Amot

micro_segments.title: Rav Yitzchak's Interpretation: The Branch Must Extend Outside the Four Amot Zone
Diagram of the Problem: Branch Position and Height Distinctions (Above vs. Below Ten Tefachim)
The Gemara's Logic: How Multiple Steps Within Four Amot Permit Access
Tosafot's Question: Why Does Suspension Above Three Tefachim Not Disqualify the Branch?

macro_segments.title: People's Use of the Branch and Its Effect on Reshus Classification

micro_segments.title: Ulla's Principle: A Platform Used by the Public Becomes Part of Reshus HaRabbim
Application to the Tree Case: Why a Well-Used Branch Cannot Serve as an Intermediate Zone

macro_segments.title: The Mishnaic Dispute: Rabbi vs. the Chachamim on Rabbinic Accessibility

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Eruv Placement Between Three and Ten Tefachim (Rabbi's Position)
Rabbi's Principle: Rabbinic Obstacles Do Not Disqualify an Eruv
Chachamim's Objection: If You Cannot Access It, It Does Not Count as an Eruv
The Gemara's Abstraction: Rabbi's Position Generalizes to Permitting D'Rabbanans at Bein Hashmashos

macro_segments.title: The Basket Case: Why an Eruv in a Suspended Basket Works Above Ten Tefachim

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Question: Why Does a Basket Above Ten Tefachim Count When a Branch Does Not?
Ravina's Solution: The Branch Itself Is Not Four by Four; Only With the Basket Does It Become So
Rabbi Meir's Principle: We Envision Space as Carved Out to Achieve Required Dimensions
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: An Eruv Must Be on a Space of Four by Four Tefachim

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Between Base and Top: When Does Four by Four Tefachim Create Reshus HaYachid?

micro_segments.title: Rashi's Distinction: Reshus HaYachid Requires Four by Four Tefachim at the Base, Not Just the Top
The Pole Case: Four by Four at the Top Only; Implications for the Basket
Rabbi Yosi ben Rabbi Yehuda: A Platform of Four by Four at Any Height Creates Reshus HaYachid
Distinguishing the Basket: Walls Descend vs. Walls Do Not Descend (Mechitza Consideration)

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation: Rebbe Yirmiyah on Basket Accessibility via Tilting

micro_segments.title: The Basket Is Different: It Can Be Tilted to Lower the Eruv Below Ten Tefachim
Two-Stage Access: Tilting Into Karmelis, Then Direct Access Without Violating Reshus Violation

macro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Teaching and the Ultimate Challenge: Do We Ever Need to Place an Eruv Physically?

micro_segments.title: Rav Shava's Radical Question: If We Can Theoretically Access It via Multiple Small Steps, Why Place It at All?
Practical Scenario: Making an Eruv for Both Yom Tov and Shabbat Across Two Days
Rabbi Zeira's Answer: The Only Requirement Is for Yom Tov Following Shabbat, When Carrying Is Prohibited
Implication: For Eruv Techumin on Friday, Physical Placement Might Not Be Required If Theoretical Access Suffices
